61eeefa86c1656aa.rule: Frame UX feedback as concrete bulleted fixes, each naming the problem then the desired behavior, and call out the single most important affordance as 'IT IS the product'.
61eeefa86c1656aa.why: Matt's dogfood reviews are dense bullet lists tying each fix to a user-behavior rationale; he isolates the core action and explicitly de-prioritizes nice-to-haves ('cherry on top', 'deferred').
61eeefa86c1656aa.when: Reviewing product/UX surfaces in Matt's voice (dogfood, feature feedback).
61eeefa86c1656aa.example_after: The main sticking action of the slide builder should be a command line that says "Ask Zerg to Build a Slide"... Don't bury it under "+ New" — it IS the product.

61eeefa86c1656aa.rule: Justify each UX recommendation with a behavioral 'why' (what the user expects or does), not just the change itself.
61eeefa86c1656aa.why: Every line in Matt's ca-org review pairs the fix with reasoning about user intent or cognitive load ('edit affordances right under your cursor', 'manipulate it the way they'd ask a junior').
61eeefa86c1656aa.when: Writing product/UX feedback in Matt's voice.
61eeefa86c1656aa.example_after: macro/summary fields at the BOTTOM, detail/edit fields at the TOP... you want the edit affordances right under your cursor, not a summary you already know.

61eeefa86c1656aa.rule: In UX reviews, anchor recommendations to how analogous tools solve it (Figma/Photoshop/CAD entity panels) and explain the underlying principle, not just the fix.
61eeefa86c1656aa.why: Matt's product feedback teaches the why by referencing established patterns and the coherence principle behind a control surface, rather than prescribing a one-off change.
61eeefa86c1656aa.when: Product/UX feedback on creation tools, canvas/builder UIs, control panels.
61eeefa86c1656aa.example_after: this kind of entity view is really common in CAD/creative tools ... figma/photoshop provide this

61eeefa86c1656aa.rule: Flag blank-by-default fields as friction: auto-fill a sensible default (e.g. 'untitled slide') so the user isn't forced to act before understanding the tool.
61eeefa86c1656aa.why: Matt treats empty required fields on load as a usability failure that puts undue onus on a new user; he also watches field padding/spacing.
61eeefa86c1656aa.when: Product/UX feedback on creation tools and forms.
61eeefa86c1656aa.example_after: on load, you should auto-fill a default title here - this places onus on the user to type something before they even understand the tool
